tweets.text:
10 free AI courses that are actually worth your time in 2026.

Not mass-produced certificate farms. These are the ones hiring managers recognize.

Bookmark this. You'll need it. 🧵👇

1/ Machine Learning by Andrew Ng (Coursera)

Still the gold standard. 60 hours. Self-paced.

Covers regression, neural networks, recommender systems. Updated for 2026 with transformer fundamentals.

Free to audit: coursera.org/learn/machine-learning-course

2/ fast.ai - Practical Deep Learning for Coders

Top-down approach. You build first, understand theory later.

7 lessons. Free. No prerequisites beyond basic Python.

Jeremy Howard teaches you to train models in the first 30 minutes.

course.fast.ai

3/ CS50 Introduction to AI with Python (Harvard/edX)

Search algorithms, knowledge, ML, neural networks, NLP.

12 weeks. Free to audit.


edx.org/learn/artificial-intelligence/harvard-university-cs50-s-introduction-to-artificial-intelligence-with-python

4/ Google AI Essentials (Coursera)

Google's own intro to AI course. 10 hours.

Prompt engineering, responsible AI, applied use cases.

Beginner friendly. Free certificate if you qualify for financial aid.

coursera.org/learn/google-ai-essentials

5/ DeepLearning.AI - Generative AI for Everyone

Andrew Ng again. But this one is for non-engineers.

Covers how LLMs work, what they can/can't do, and how to evaluate AI projects.

3 hours total. Free to audit.

coursera.org/learn/generative-ai-for-everyone

6/ Hugging Face NLP Course

Hands-on transformers course. Build real NLP pipelines.

Tokenization, fine-tuning, model evaluation.

Free. Open source. Updated monthly.

huggingface.co/learn/nlp-course

7/ MIT 6.S191 - Introduction to Deep Learning

MIT's official deep learning course. Full video lectures on YouTube.

TensorFlow based. Covers CNNs, RNNs, GANs, reinforcement learning.

Free forever.

introtodeeplearning.com

8/ Stanford CS229 - Machine Learning (Full Course on YouTube)

The Stanford ML course that started it all.

20 lectures. Andrew Ng era.

More math-heavy than Coursera version. Great if you want the theory depth.


youtube.com/playlist?list=PLoROMvodv4rMiGQp3WXShtMGgzqpfVfbU

9/ Kaggle Learn - Intro to ML, Deep Learning, NLP

Bite-sized interactive courses. Each takes 4 hours.

You write code directly in your browser. No setup.

Perfect for weekend learning.

www.kaggle.com/learn

10/ Elements of AI (University of Helsinki)

750,000+ students enrolled. Covers AI fundamentals without code.

Philosophy, probability, machine learning basics.

Genuinely enjoyable. Free.

elementsofai.com

That's the list. Bookmark it. Share it with someone who needs it.

If you're building skills to stand out in the job market, start with a profile that actually represents you.

cvin.bio

7 free blockchain developer programs you can access right now.

No paid bootcamps. No $2,000 courses. Just free, legit programs from actual protocols.

Save this thread. 🧵👇

1/ Alchemy University - Ethereum Developer Bootcamp

Full Solidity curriculum. Build smart contracts from scratch.

6 weeks. Completely free. Certificate on completion.

Used by 100,000+ developers.

university.alchemy.com

Learn to build hybrid smart contracts with oracles.

Covers Chainlink VRF, Price Feeds, Automation.

Free. Live cohorts with mentorship.

3/ Solana Bootcamp by Solana Foundation

Rust + Anchor framework. Build programs on Solana.

Project-based learning. Free.

The fastest blockchain to build on, with the best docs.

4/ Consensys Academy - Blockchain Developer Program

Ethereum fundamentals, Solidity, Truffle, smart contract security.

Self-paced. Free tier available.

From the team that built MetaMask.

5/ Web3 University (by Alchemy)

Tutorial-style courses. Build an NFT minting dapp, DeFi protocols, DAOs.

Each project takes 1-3 hours. Free.

web3.university

6/ Cyfrin Updraft (by Patrick Collins)

The most comprehensive Solidity course available. Free.

32 hours of content. Foundry-based. Security-focused.

Patrick Collins is the best Solidity teacher on the internet.

updraft.cyfrin.io

7/ Polkadot Blockchain Academy

Free online materials covering Substrate, Rust, parachain development.

More advanced. Great if you want multi-chain expertise.

polkadot.academy

These programs are used by developers working in Web3 right now.

We track 17,000+ tech jobs including Web3 roles. Browse them here:

cvin.bio/jobs

NEW: The 2026 Python Roadmap is here.

Stop wasting time on tutorial hell. This is the exact 4-week plan to land clear results.

Save this for your next career move. 🧵

Week 1: Fundamentals

→ Variables, data types, loops, conditionals
→ Functions, scope, basic error handling

Use: Python.org official tutorial (free)
Practice: Exercism.org Python track (free)

Spend 2-3 hours/day. Don't skip the exercises.

Exercism

Week 2: Data Structures + Problem Solving

→ Lists, dicts, sets, tuples
→ List comprehensions, slicing
→ File I/O, JSON handling

Use: Automate the Boring Stuff (free online)
Practice: LeetCode Easy problems in Python


AutomateTheBoringStuff

Week 3: Libraries + Real Projects

→ requests, BeautifulSoup (web scraping)
→ pandas (data analysis)
→ Flask or FastAPI (web APIs)

Project: Build a web scraper that saves data to CSV.
Project: Build a REST API with 3 endpoints.

Put both on GitHub.

cvin.bio

Week 4: Portfolio + Job Readiness

→ Git basics (commit, push, branch, PR)
→ Write a README for each project
→ Deploy one project (Render, Railway, or Vercel)
→ Build a simple portfolio page

Apply to 10 junior Python roles. Don't wait until you feel ready.

cvin.bio

Resources that are 100% free:

→ Python Docs
→ AutomateTheBoringStuff
→ Exercism
→ RealPython (many free articles)
→ FreeCodeCamp Python course on YouTube
→ Kaggle (Python + Pandas)

No excuses. Just start.

The biggest mistake people make: tutorial hell.

Stop watching. Start building. Break things. Google the error. Fix it.

That's how every professional developer learned.

Share this with someone who's been "planning to learn Python" for months.

7 free tools that replaced a $500/month subscription stack.

Not joking. Every one of these is free or has a generous free tier.

Save this. Your wallet will thank you. 🧵👇

1/ Cursor - AI-powered code editor

Free tier gives you autocomplete + chat with your codebase.

Replaces: GitHub Copilot ($10/mo)

cursor.com

2/ Supabase - Backend as a Service

Postgres database, auth, storage, realtime, edge functions.

Free tier: 500MB database, 1GB storage, 50,000 monthly active users.

Replaces: Firebase + Auth0 ($25+/mo)

supabase.com

3/ Vercel - Frontend deployment

Deploy React/Next.js with zero config. Free SSL, CDN, preview deploys.

Free for personal projects and hobby use.

Replaces: Heroku + Netlify paid tiers ($7+/mo)

vercel.com

4/ Excalidraw - Whiteboard for diagrams

Clean, hand-drawn style diagrams. Real-time collaboration.

Completely free. Open source.

Replaces: Miro, Figma for diagramming ($12+/mo)

excalidraw.com

5/ Plausible/Umami - Privacy-first analytics

Umami is self-hosted, 100% free. Plausible has a paid plan but Umami doesn't.

Replaces: Google Analytics (free but privacy-invasive) or Mixpanel ($25+/mo)

umami.is

6/ Resend - Email API

Beautiful developer experience. React Email for templates.

3,000 emails/month free. Way simpler than SendGrid.

Replaces: SendGrid/Mailgun on paid tiers

resend.com

7/ Linear - Project management

Free for teams up to 250 issues. Fastest PM tool we've ever used.

Replaces: Jira ($7.75/user/mo) and the suffering that comes with it.

linear.app

Total savings: $500+/month

The best tools in 2026 are either free or have free tiers good enough for 90% of use cases.

Share this with a founder or freelancer who's overpaying for SaaS.

Remote job hunting in 2026 - a complete guide.

Where to actually find remote jobs. Red flags to avoid. How to negotiate.

Everything we wish someone told us.

🧵👇

Where to find real remote jobs:

→ We Work Remotely
→ Remote OK
→ Himalayas
→ Remotive
→ AngelList/Wellfound
→ cvin.bio/jobs (we track 17,000+ roles)

Avoid: LinkedIn "remote" posts that say "must be in NYC" in the description.

Red flags in remote job listings:

→ "Must overlap 8 hours with US timezone" = not remote
→ No salary range = lowball incoming
→ "Startup culture" = always online
→ "Camera on" daily standups = surveillance

cvin.bio/jobs

How to stand out applying remotely:

1/ Record a 60-second Loom intro
2/ Show timezone overlap in your cover note
3/ Mention async tools you use (Notion, Linear, Slack)
4/ Include a link to your work, not a PDF

Video cuts through 500+ applications.

cvin.bio/jobs

Negotiating remote compensation:

→ Never accept the first offer
→ Ask "Is this adjusted for location?"
→ If they adjust for cost of living, negotiate perks instead
→ Get the remote policy in writing

"Permanent remote" means nothing without a contract clause.

cvin.bio/jobs

Remote work truth in 2026:

63% of companies use hybrid as default.
Fully remote roles dropped 23% YoY.
But remote-first companies grow faster.

The filter: apply to remote-first, not "allows remote."

More data:
cvin.bio/remote-talent-report

The job market is harder than ever. But the tools available are better than ever.

Use them. Share this with someone still doing it the old way.

cvin.bio

You applied to 150 jobs and got 0 interviews.

It's not your experience. It's your PDF.

We built a CV parsing engine. Here's exactly what happens when you upload your resume to an ATS. 🧵👇

1/ The Two-Column Collapse

Older systems parse PDFs top-to-bottom, left-to-right. They don't understand columns.

If 'Skills' is next to 'Work History', the ATS mashes them into garbled text.

Instant rejection. Before a human ever sees it.

cvin.bio/jobs

2/ Invisible SVG Contact Info

Custom vector icons (mail icon, phone icon) instead of raw text? OCR libraries skip them.

Without 'Email:' as a text anchor, parsers fail to map the string.

Your email literally disappears from the database.

cvin.bio/jobs

3/ Keyword Matching is Boolean

Legacy ATS scoring is exact string matching. Not an LLM.

'Node.js' vs 'NodeJS' = different keywords. Your score drops.

We had to build fuzzy-matching arrays mapping 40+ variations just to handle this.

cvin.bio/jobs

4/ DOCX is a ZIP of XML

Upload .docx = upload an XML tree. Complex nested tables = deeply layered XML.

If the expected node path fails, the parser throws a silent error.

The recruiter sees: empty fields.

cvin.bio/jobs

5/ The Real Numbers

75% of resumes never reach a human.
Average time on each resume: 7.4 seconds.
85% of Fortune 500 companies use ATS.

The filter wasn't built to find the best candidates. It was built to reduce volume. You are collateral damage.

cvin.bio/jobs

The fix: stop trusting the black box.

Send a structured, live URL instead of a PDF.

No parsing. No garbled text. No silent failures.

Get your free profile link:

cvin.bio

cvin.bio

What hiring managers actually look at.

10 things that matter more than your GPA or your degree.

Save this. 🧵

1. Your GitHub activity.

Not how many stars you have. Your contribution history.

Consistent commits over months beat one viral repo that you never touched again. They want to see that you actually write code regularly.

2. Live project links.

A deployed app beats a PDF portfolio every single time.

If a hiring manager can click a link and see your work running in their browser, you are already ahead of 90% of applicants.

3. How you describe your work.

"Built a REST API" says nothing. "Reduced API response time by 40% by implementing caching" says everything.

The difference between a junior and a senior resume is specificity.

4. Your most recent role.

They spend 80% of their time reading about your last 2 years. Everything before that gets a quick scan at best.

Front-load the resume with your freshest, most relevant work.

5. Technical writing.

Blog posts. READMEs. Documentation.

Clarity of thought is hard to fake. If you can explain a complex system in plain language, that tells them you actually understand it.

6. Side projects that solve real problems.

Not tutorial clones. Something you built because you actually needed it.

A budget tracker you use daily is worth more than 10 todo apps you built for practice.

7. How you talk about tradeoffs.

Every senior hire gets evaluated on judgment, not just technical skill.

"We chose Postgres over Mongo because our data was highly relational" shows you think about decisions, not just execute them.

8. Cultural signals.

Open source contributions. Community involvement. Conference talks, even small ones.

Signs that you care about the craft beyond your 9-to-5. That signal is hard to manufacture.

PDFs get filtered. Links get clicked.

Turn your CV into a live website in 30 seconds. No code. No design skills. Just upload and share.

cvin.bio

How to go from zero to hired as a developer in 90 days.

No CS degree needed. No bootcamp required. Just a clear plan and consistent effort.

This is the path that works the most. 🧵

Days 1-10. Pick one language and stick with it.

JavaScript or Python. Nothing else.

The biggest mistake beginners make is jumping between languages every week. You do not need to know five languages. You need to know one well.

Days 11-25. Build something real every single day.

Not tutorials. Not following along with videos. Actually building things.

A weather widget. A bookmark manager. Small, complete projects that you finish and ship.

Days 26-40. Learn Git and deploy everything.

Every project goes on GitHub. Every project gets deployed live.

Vercel, Netlify, or Railway. It does not matter where. What matters is that someone can click a link and see it working.

Days 41-55. Build one project that solves a real problem.

Not a tutorial clone. Something you or someone you know actually needs.

A budget tracker. A job application log. A habit tracker. Real problems make real portfolio pieces.

Days 56-65. Read other people's code.

Open source projects on GitHub. Pull requests from experienced developers. Study how they structure things.

You learn more from reading good code than from writing bad code.

Days 66-75. Start applying before you feel ready.

You will never feel ready. That feeling does not go away even after years of experience.

Apply to 10 jobs a day. Treat the job search itself as a skill you are practicing.

Days 76-85. Prepare for interviews differently.

Most junior roles care more about your projects and how you explain your decisions than LeetCode scores.

Practice explaining your code decisions out loud. That is what interviews actually test.

Days 86-90. Follow up on everything.

Most candidates never follow up. A simple email two days after an interview puts you ahead of 80% of applicants.

Be specific about something you discussed. Show genuine interest.

90 days is not a lot of time. But it is enough to build a foundation that gets you in the door.

Skip the PDF. Turn your CV into a website that hiring managers can actually browse and share.

cvin.bio

10 portfolio projects that actually get you interviews.

Not todo apps. Not calculator clones. Projects that make hiring managers pause and click.

Save this list. 🧵

1. A personal finance dashboard.

Track income, expenses, and savings goals with charts and data visualization.

This shows you can handle state management, work with data, and build something people would actually use every day.

2. A job application tracker.

Log applications, interview stages, follow-ups, and outcomes. Include simple analytics.

Every hiring manager knows the pain of job searching. They instantly relate to this project.

3. A real-time chat application.

WebSockets, authentication, message history. Does not need to be Slack. Just needs to work.

Real-time features immediately set you apart from candidates who only built static pages.

4. An API that other people can actually use.

Build a REST API for something useful. Document it properly. Add rate limiting and error handling.

This proves you understand backend fundamentals beyond just following a tutorial.

5. A markdown blog engine.

Write posts in markdown, render them as styled pages, support tags and search.

This proves you understand content management, routing, and SEO basics. All things companies care about.

6. A browser extension.

A tab manager. A reading time estimator. A color picker. Something small but useful.

Extensions show you understand browser APIs and distribution. Most candidates never build one. That is the advantage.

7. A data scraper with a clean frontend.

Scrape public data from an interesting source. Display it in a clean, searchable interface.

This combines backend skills, data processing, and frontend presentation in one project.

8. An open source contribution.

Not a whole project. Just one meaningful pull request to an established repository.

This demonstrates you can read existing code, follow contribution guidelines, and work with a team.

The best portfolio is not the one with the most projects. It is the one people can actually see.

Turn your CV into a shareable website with live project links. Takes 30 seconds. Free.

cvin.bio

How to negotiate 40% more salary in tech.

Most people accept the first offer. That single decision can cost you $30K or more per year.

Here is the exact playbook. 🧵

Never share your current salary.

In many places it is illegal for them to even ask. If they push, say your research shows the market range for this role is a specific number.

The person who names a number first usually loses.

Research the actual market rate before the call.

Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, and Blind are useful for this. Look at the specific company, level, and location.

Walk in with three numbers. Your floor. Your target. Your stretch.

Let them make the first offer. Always.

If they ask your expectations, give a range where your target is the bottom number.

Saying 140 to 160 means they may offer 140. Say 160 to 180 if you want 160.

When you get the offer, do not react immediately.

Say thank you and that you need a few days to review the full package. Even if the number is amazing.

Urgency is their tool. Time is yours.

Negotiate the whole package, not just base salary.

Signing bonus. Equity. Remote work days. Learning budget. PTO. Title.

Sometimes a better title now means a $20K raise at your next job.

Use competing offers if you have them.

You do not need to name the company. Just say you are evaluating other opportunities in a certain range.

Competition creates urgency on their side. Use it respectfully.

Practice the conversation out loud before the call.

Negotiation is a skill. It feels awkward because most people never practice it.

One rehearsal with a friend removes 90% of the anxiety.

Know when to stop.

Push too hard and you start the job with a damaged relationship. Get to a number you are happy with and accept gracefully.

The best negotiators know the line between confident and greedy.

One negotiation conversation can be worth $100K or more over the life of your career.

Before you walk in, make sure your profile is a website, not a PDF. Upload your CV and get a live link in 30 seconds.

cvin.bio

What nobody tells you about working at a startup.

After talking to hundreds of people who left big companies for startups, these patterns kept coming up.

Read this before you make the jump. 🧵

The equity is probably worth nothing.

90% of startups fail. Even the ones that succeed often dilute early employees so heavily that the payout is disappointing.

Do not take a $40K pay cut for equity unless you can genuinely afford to lose it.

You will wear every hat whether you signed up for it or not.

Hired as a frontend developer. Now also doing DevOps, customer support, writing docs, and interviewing candidates.

Some people love this. Some people burn out in six months.

The culture is the founder.

At companies under 50 people, culture is not a set of values on a wall. It is whatever the founder does on a bad Tuesday.

Meet the founder before you accept. Watch how they treat the most junior person in the room.

Speed matters more than quality at most early-stage startups.

You may ship code you are not proud of. You may skip tests because the deadline is tomorrow.

If you need structure and code review processes, a Series A startup may not be for you.

Remote startups can be incredibly lonely.

No office. A Slack channel with 12 people. A weekly standup on Zoom.

If you draw energy from being around people, make sure the startup has strong rituals around connection.

Your manager may have never managed anyone before.

First-time managers at startups are the norm, not the exception. You may need to manage up, ask for feedback yourself, and create your own growth plan.

Nobody is coming to mentor you. You have to seek it out.

The upside is real though.

You learn faster at startups than anywhere else. Two years at a good startup can give you five years of big-company experience.

The learning curve is steep but the compound effect on your career is massive.

Ask these questions before you accept.

What is your runway. How many months of cash do you have. What does the cap table look like for my equity. Who are your biggest customers.

If they dodge these questions, that tells you everything.

Startups are not better or worse than big companies. They are different.

Wherever you end up, make your CV a website that anyone can open with one tap. No downloads. No attachments.

cvin.bio

"We are a family."

If they use "family" to steal your weekends, you are in a cult.

Find companies that respect your time at cvin.bio

Giving 3 years to a company only to be laid off by an AI script.

Automation is for code, not empathy.

Connect directly with real hiring managers at cvin.bio

0.5% equity and no salary does not pay the rent.

Engineers: your skills are highly valuable. Stop working for free.

Get paid what you are actually worth at cvin.bio

"Can you just join the Zoom from bed?"

When people are sick, they need to rest.

Find tech teams that respect your health at cvin.bio

The job market in 2026, in 10 numbers.

None of this is opinion.

75% of resumes are rejected before a human sees them.

Not after review. Before.

7.4 seconds.

That's the average a recruiter spends on the ones that do get through.

250 people apply per opening now.

You're not being evaluated. You're being sorted.

Bad hires cost companies $240k on average.

But they still won't spend 20 minutes reading your actual work.

85% of jobs get filled through networking.

The application form exists to make you feel like you had a fair shot.

63% of "entry-level" listings ask for 3+ years.

Entry level for who.

92 million jobs gone by 2030.

Not reshuffled. Gone.

6 seconds.

That's how long your CV has before it's in the reject pile. Page two doesn't exist.

72% of candidates get ghosted after a final round interview.

Five rounds of prep. Then nothing.

3 out of 4 resumes get thrown out by software.

You're not losing to better candidates. You're losing to a parser.

PDFs get filtered.
Links get clicked.

insights.text:
We analyzed 6,000+ job postings on our board. Backend dev is the most posted role but almost every listing now casually asks for AI experience. Not AI jobs. Regular backend jobs.

AI is becoming a checkbox, not a specialty.

Full breakdown: cvin.bio/jobs

Two years ago every company was hiring engineers to build. Now they need people to keep it running.

DevOps and infrastructure roles grew 40% on our board compared to last quarter.

The builders got hired. Now they need maintainers.

cvin.bio/jobs

We looked at what skills show up most across 6,000+ job listings.

Python and TypeScript dominate everything.
Go is quietly climbing.
Java is fading from startups.

Degrees keep mattering less. Portfolios keep mattering more.

cvin.bio/jobs

The thing that keeps jumping out from our data: barely any junior roles.

Most postings are senior or above. If you're early career, the normal path of apply and hope barely exists anymore.

You need to be found, not found in a pile.

cvin.bio/layoffs-report

Way more companies listing salary now than even a year ago.

The holdouts are mostly big legacy companies still posting "competitive."

Everyone else figured out that hiding the number just costs them applicants.

cvin.bio/jobs

One thing we didn't expect from our job board data: how many hiring managers just google candidates before looking at the resume.

If nothing comes up, you get skipped.

A PDF in your downloads folder doesn't show up on Google. A live link does.

cvin.bio

Most jobs on our board say "remote" but then you read the description.

"Must overlap 4 hours with US EST."
"Async-first team."
"Occasional travel required."

Remote in 2026 means: "we don't have an office for you but we still need you online."

cvin.bio/remote-talent-report

Interesting pattern from our data: companies that list salary ranges get 3.2x more applications than those that don't.

Transparency isn't just ethical. It's better recruiting.

cvin.bio/jobs

Top 5 fastest-growing job categories on our board this quarter:

1. AI/ML Engineer (+67%)
2. DevOps/Platform (+40%)
3. Security Engineer (+35%)
4. Data Engineer (+28%)
5. Developer Relations (+22%)

Frontend is flat. Backend is steady. AI is exploding.

cvin.bio/jobs

We track which companies on our board hire the fastest.

The pattern: startups under 50 people close roles in 2-3 weeks. Companies over 500 take 45-90 days for the same role.

If speed matters to you, go smaller.

cvin.bio/jobs

Expecting 8 years of experience for an entry-level role is wild.

Skip the ghost jobs and the 7-round interview circuses.

Match directly with hiring companies at cvin.bio

The classic 2:14 AM Saturday "emergency".

A lack of planning by leadership is not an emergency for your weekend.

High-quality teams are hiring async-first developers right now at cvin.bio

Nothing screams "terrible management" quite like mouse-tracking software.

Remote work requires trust. If they have to track your mouse, leave.

Find remote companies that respect you at cvin.bio

React is still the most requested frontend framework on our board. By far.

But Next.js is now mentioned in 60% of those React listings.

Plain React without a meta-framework is becoming a minority.

cvin.bio/jobs

Rust jobs grew 45% on our board YoY.

Most are in: blockchain, infrastructure, systems programming.

Average salary: 15% higher than equivalent Go or Python roles.

If you're looking for a high-use skill to learn in 2026, Rust is it.

cvin.bio/jobs

"Years of experience" is the most meaningless filter in job listings.

We see the data: companies asking for 5+ years regularly hire people with 2-3 years who have strong portfolios.

The number is a wish list. Not a requirement.

Show the work, not the years.

cvin.bio

Companies with 4+ interview rounds take 3x longer to fill roles than those with 2 rounds.

And they don't hire better candidates. They just lose the good ones to faster companies.

Our data proves: speed wins.

cvin.bio/jobs

We looked at which tech stacks appear together most often:

→ TypeScript + React + Node.js (47% of listings)
→ Python + FastAPI + PostgreSQL (23%)
→ Go + gRPC + Kubernetes (12%)
→ Rust + Solana + TypeScript (8%)

Pick a stack. Go deep. Breadth comes later.

cvin.bio/jobs

The most underrated job search strategy according to our data:

Apply in the first 48 hours of a listing going live.

Listings older than 2 weeks already have 200+ applicants. New ones have under 20.

Speed beats perfection.

cvin.bio/jobs

Job listings with the word "passionate" are 2.3x more likely to offer below-market salary.

When they can't compete on pay, they compete on guilt.

"We need someone passionate" = "We need someone who won't ask about money."

cvin.bio/jobs

We looked at which job boards our companies cross-post to:

LinkedIn: 89%
Indeed: 61%
Wellfound: 34%
Greenhouse public: 28%

The takeaway: don't apply on LinkedIn where you compete with 500 people. Find the company's direct application page.

cvin.bio/jobs

TypeScript adoption in job listings: 2022 vs 2026

2022: 34% of JS roles mentioned TypeScript
2026: 78% of JS roles require TypeScript

If you write JavaScript without TypeScript in 2026, you're disqualifying yourself from most roles.

cvin.bio/jobs

We track job listings that mention "AI experience preferred" but aren't AI-specific roles.

2024: 8% of listings
2025: 19% of listings
2026: 41% of listings

AI literacy is becoming like Excel was in 2010: not optional.

cvin.bio/jobs

From our data: companies that include salary ranges in job listings fill positions 28% faster than those that don't.

Vague compensation descriptions ("competitive", "based on experience") are the #1 reason candidates abandon applications.

Transparency wins.

cvin.bio/jobs

78% of dev job listings now mention cloud certifications as a plus.

AWS: 52% of mentions
GCP: 28%
Azure: 20%

The meta: cloud certs are the new degree. They're not required, but they separate equal candidates.

cvin.bio/jobs

Our board shows 17,000+ open roles right now.

But here's the thing: 60% of them are from companies with fewer than 200 employees.

Stop only applying to FAANG. The best roles are at companies you haven't heard of yet.

cvin.bio/jobs

Docker is mentioned in 71% of backend job listings on our board.

It used to be a DevOps skill. Now it's a basic requirement for any backend developer.

If you can't containerize your app, learn it this weekend. It takes 3 hours.

cvin.bio/jobs

GraphQL adoption in job listings peaked in 2024 and has been declining.

REST + tRPC is the new standard for most startups.

GraphQL isn't dying. It's just no longer the default choice.

cvin.bio/jobs

Average time from first interview to offer in our data:

Startups (<50 people): 12 days
Mid-size (50-500): 28 days
Enterprise (500+): 52 days

The best candidates get 3-4 offers. They go with whoever moves fastest.

cvin.bio/jobs

Surprising finding: the word "ninja" in job listings has dropped 94% since 2022.

"Rockstar" dropped 87%.

"Builder" is up 340%.

Companies finally learned that cringe job titles repel senior talent.

cvin.bio/jobs

We analyzed which skills have the highest salary premium above baseline:

1. Kubernetes: +18%
2. Rust: +15%
3. ML/AI: +14%
4. Security: +12%
5. Staff-level architecture: +25%

Specialization pays. Generalism gets you in the door.

cvin.bio/jobs

From our data: companies that require cover letters get 45% fewer applications.

And they don't get better candidates. They just get fewer.

The cover letter is dying. Companies that still require them are self-selecting for smaller pools.

cvin.bio/jobs

How job listing language changed since 2023:

"Fast-paced" → down 31%
"Work hard play hard" → down 67%
"Competitive salary" → down 23%
"Flexible hours" → up 45%
"Async" → up 89%
"4-day work week" → up 210%

Language tells you where the market is going.

cvin.bio/jobs

engagement.text:
Someone on Reddit shared this. 2,000 upvotes in a day.

Work of 3 people. $53k. No raise.

Manager promised 15%. Got 1.75%.

Left his keys on the desk. Walked out by 10am.

Nobody was surprised.

Has this happened to you? Drop the real number below.

cvin.bio

Job hunting in 2026:

45 minutes filling a form that already had your CV.
One call. Two interviews. Take-home task.

Two weeks later: nothing. Not a rejection. Just silence.

What's the longest you've been ghosted after an interview?

cvin.bio

What's the worst interview question you've ever been asked?

We'll start: "If you were a kitchen utensil, which one would you be and why?"

This was for a senior engineering role.

Drop yours below 👇

One sentence that changed how I think about careers:

"Your company would post your job opening before your obituary."

What's the one sentence that changed your career perspective? Reply below.

Companies say they can't find talent.

They fired 3 people. Told 1 to cover it all.

Talent shortages at a 17-year high. Employee engagement at a 10-year low.

What's the most understaffed situation you've been in? 👇

cvin.bio

Annual review.

Manager: "We value you."
HR: "Budget constraints."
Letter: 2.1% raise.
Inflation: 3.8%.

Effective pay cut dressed up as a raise.

What was your last "raise" percentage? Real numbers only. 👇

What's the one tool you can't live without as a developer?

Not the obvious ones. Something most people don't know about.

We'll compile the best answers into a thread later this week.

cvin.bio/jobs

Controversial opinion: cover letters are dead and everyone knows it but nobody wants to say it.

Agree or disagree? And if you disagree, when was the last time a cover letter actually got you the job?

cvin.bio/jobs

What's one thing you learned the hard way in your career that you wish someone had told you on day one?

Ours: "Don't stay loyal to a company that wouldn't stay loyal to you."

Yours? 👇

cvin.bio/jobs

Your CV goes to:
1. Email inbox.
2. ATS keyword filter.
3. 6-second skim.
4. Pile.

None of these care about your actual work.

What's the most ridiculous ATS rejection you've experienced?

cvin.bio

Monitoring restroom time. Are we running companies or prisons?

Treating adults like children is exactly why top talent leaves.

Find remote-first companies that measure output at cvin.bio

"Unlimited PTO" is the biggest scam in tech.

It usually means: unlimited days off, as long as you never actually use them.

Find teams that value actual work-life balance at cvin.bio

An 8-hour take-home project just to get rejected.

Companies are using interview assignments as free consulting.

Skip the ridiculous hiring hoops and drop your resume on cvin.bio

The real math of staying loyal to one company:

Year 1: market rate.
Year 4: budget freeze.

Meanwhile, someone hired externally gets 20% more than you on day one.

How much did your salary go up by staying vs. switching? Share the numbers.

cvin.bio

Hot take: the 8-hour workday is theater.

Most people do 4-5 hours of actual productive work. The rest is meetings, context switching, and performative busyness.

Agree? Or am I wrong?

Drop your actual productive hours below.

cvin.bio/jobs

What's the biggest red flag you've seen in a job listing?

We'll start: "We're like a family here."

It always means: unpaid overtime, no boundaries, and emotional manipulation.

Your turn 👇

cvin.bio/jobs

If you could give one piece of advice to someone entering tech in 2026, what would it be?

Ours: Learn to ship. A finished ugly project > 10 unfinished beautiful ones.

Drop yours below.

cvin.bio/jobs

Recruiter called. Said it was urgent. Great fit.

Three interviews in two weeks.

Radio silence for a month.

Then: "Hey, are you still exploring opportunities?"

Tag a recruiter who needs to see this. 😂

cvin.bio

What did you google today as a "senior" developer?

We'll start: "how to center a div"

No judgment zone 👇

cvin.bio/jobs

Entry level used to mean you could learn on the job.

Now it means: want someone senior, not paying senior rates, not willing to train.

When did "entry level" stop meaning entry level for you?

cvin.bio

72% of resumes are rejected before a human sees them.

But here's the question: has improving your resume format ever actually gotten you more interviews?

Or is networking still the only thing that works? Honest answers only.

cvin.bio/jobs

Name a company that ghosted you after a final round interview.

I won't judge. But other job seekers deserve to know which companies respect candidates and which don't.

👇

cvin.bio/jobs

What's the most you've ever been lowballed?

I saw someone offered $45k for a role listed at $80-100k because they "didn't have enough leadership experience."

The role was mid-level individual contributor.

Quick poll: how many applications did it take for your last job?

→ Under 10
→ 10-50
→ 50-200
→ 200+

The talent shortage isn't real.

What's real: below-market pay, poor management, no flexibility.

The talent moved somewhere that treats it better.

Are you actively planning to leave your current role? No judgment. Just curious.

cvin.bio

What's the best career decision you ever made?

Ours: leaving comfortable jobs to build something from scratch. Terrifying. Best thing we ever did.

Yours?

→ AI/ML

Unpopular opinion: remote work is better for productivity but worse for career growth.

The people who get promoted are the ones who are visible. Not the ones who ship the most.

cvin.bio/jobs

She asked for market rate. They said the budget was fixed.

Two weeks later the same role was listed again. 25% higher than what she asked for.

The budget wasn't fixed. They just didn't want to pay her.

Has this happened to you? 🤦

cvin.bio

What's the most useful thing you've ever built as a side project?

Doesn't matter how small. What solved a real problem for you?

We'll start: a CLI tool that bulk-renames files based on regex patterns. Use it daily.

cvin.bio/jobs

The worst career advice you've ever received?

We'll start: "Just be patient and your hard work will get noticed."

3 years later: it didn't.

Your worst advice? 👇

cvin.bio/jobs

What's a hill you'll die on about tech careers?

Ours: nobody should have to do a 6-round interview process for any role. If you can't evaluate someone in 3 rounds, the problem is your process.

Yours?

cvin.bio/jobs

If Sunday evenings fill you with dread, that's not laziness.

It's your body telling you the deal isn't right.

The commute you hate. The manager you avoid. The Slack you mute.

On a scale of 1-10, how do you feel about Monday morning right now?

cvin.bio

"Listed as 'remote' for visibility."

It is actually an onsite position.

This is wild. And companies wonder why candidates have trust issues.

Your CV should work harder than their job descriptions.

cvin.bio

Oracle laid off 30,000 people.

Then hired a new CFO and gave her a $29.7 million pay package.

This is the job market you're competing in. Your CV needs to be sharper than ever.

cvin.bio

Interviewer: "Why is there a gap in your CV?"

Candidate: "Why is this role still open after 10 months?"

Fair question.

Make sure your profile doesn't give them reasons to question you. One link. Your whole story.

cvin.bio

If this is how companies think about hiring people then God help us.

The hiring process makes no sense. But your profile doesn't have to be part of the chaos.

cvin.bio

HR: "We decided to go with another candidate who aligns with our needs."

*Job is reposted on LinkedIn the next day*

We've all been there.

Don't let a bad process define you. Your work speaks — give it a home.

cvin.bio

3 months of applying into the void.

Turns out the resume had one disqualifying element the whole time.

ATS systems are brutal. Make sure your profile is clean, complete, and clickable.

cvin.bio

"Why do you want this job?"

"To be transparent, it's for the money."

Honesty hits different. But make sure your profile performs for you even when you can't.

cvin.bio

This is what the hiring manager wrote about your interview.

"Seems like a nice person but seems too passive."

You never see it. You just get "we went in a different direction."

Make your first impression impossible to dismiss.

cvin.bio

Applying for jobs in the middle of a recession.

We're all this dog right now.

At least make sure your profile isn't adding to the chaos.

cvin.bio

"Just wake up, go to any company, and start working."

"If they call the police, go to the police station and start working there too."

We're tired of sending CVs. At least make yours a website, not a PDF.

cvin.bio

"Tell me about a conflict at your last company and how you resolved it."

*thinking about the 47 things I can't say out loud*

Interviews are a performance. Your online profile shouldn't be.

cvin.bio

When a dishwashing job asks for 3 professional references.

This job market is unhinged.

Don't let unhinged hiring processes waste your time. One link. Your full profile. Done.
